trueFeatures: It is often in 4/4 and often follows a 12-bar chord pattern.
Blue notes, call and response and repeated riffs are common.
Improvisation is an important part of the style.
MC-0001.falseFeature: It is a staged work combining songs, spoken dialogue, acting and dance.

trueFeatures: Syncopation and improvisation are common features.
Instrumental solos and distinctive harmony are often heard.
Saxophone, trumpet, piano, bass and drum kit are common instruments.
MC-0002.falseFeature: It is a staged work combining songs, spoken dialogue, acting and dance.

trueFeatures: It often has lively syncopated or off-beat rhythms.
A strong dance pulse and prominent percussion are common.
Bongos, guiro, castanets or claves may be used.
MC-0003.falseFeature: Distorted electric-guitar riffs and a heavy amplified sound are central.

trueFeatures: It is a staged dramatic work with songs, acting and usually dance.
Spoken dialogue may be used between songs.
A band or orchestra accompanies songs that develop the story or characters.
MC-0004.falseFeature: Sound is made by buzzing the lips into a cup-shaped or funnel-shaped mouthpiece.

trueFeatures: Memorable songs and clear hooks are important.
Verses and choruses are often repeated.
Lead vocals are often supported by guitars, keyboards, bass and drum kit.
MC-0005.falseFeature: It is performed by tuned steel pans associated with Trinidad and Tobago.

trueFeatures: It has a strong driving beat and prominent amplified sound.
Electric guitar, bass guitar, drum kit and vocals are common.
Guitar riffs and distortion are often important.
MC-0006.falseFeature: It is a large ensemble divided into strings, woodwind, brass and percussion.

trueFeatures: It is a lively American style with roots in blues, gospel and country.
A strong backbeat and walking bass are common.
Riffs and a 12-bar chord pattern are often used.
MC-0007.falseFeature: It is a dance in simple triple time with a ONE-two-three accent pattern.

trueFeatures: It includes traditional songs, dances and instrumental music from Scotland.
Fiddle, accordion and bagpipes are common instruments.
Marches, reels, jigs, strathspeys and waltzes are common dance types.
MC-0008.falseFeature: Sound is made by buzzing the lips into a cup-shaped or funnel-shaped mouthpiece.

trueFeatures: Each later pitch is higher than the one before it.
A melody travels from a lower register towards a higher one.
The upward movement may happen by step or by leap.
MC-0009.falseFeature: Each later pitch is lower than the one before it.

trueFeatures: Two or more different notes sound together.
It creates harmony and can support or accompany a melody.
A three-note example is called a triad.
MC-0010.falseFeature: It is the interval between notes with the same letter name.

trueFeatures: It is a movement from one chord to another.
A series of these movements creates a chord progression.
Related chords often share notes from the same scale.
MC-0011.falseFeature: The tempo decreases so that the beat moves less quickly than before.

trueFeatures: Each later pitch is lower than the one before it.
A melody travels from a higher register towards a lower one.
The downward movement may happen by step or by leap.
MC-0012.falseFeature: Each later pitch is higher than the one before it.

trueFeatures: Notes clash when they sound together.
The sound creates tension or instability.
It may resolve to a smoother, more settled chord.
MC-0013.falseFeature: All performers sing or play the same melody at the same pitch.

trueFeatures: Musical material is created during the performance.
Chords, scales or an agreed structure may guide the performer.
It is common in jazz and blues.
MC-0014.falseFeature: A written dot and curved arch tells the performer to hold a note or rest longer.

trueFeatures: The melody moves between notes that are not next to each other.
One or more neighbouring scale notes are skipped.
It creates a larger pitch movement than a step.
MC-0015.falseFeature: The melody moves only between neighbouring notes of the scale.

trueFeatures: An opening phrase is followed by a responding phrase.
The two phrases are often similar in length or rhythm.
The response may complete, contrast with or copy the opening.
MC-0016.falseFeature: One short pattern repeats continuously beneath the music.

trueFeatures: A musical idea returns without changing its pitch pattern or rhythm.
It may happen in a melody or rhythm.
It may also shape the structure of a piece.
MC-0017.falseFeature: The repeated phrase begins on a new higher or lower pitch.

trueFeatures: A melodic phrase is repeated immediately.
The repeat begins on a higher or lower pitch.
The phrase keeps its shape while its pitch level changes.
MC-0018.falseFeature: The idea repeats without any change to its pitch pattern.

trueFeatures: The melody moves between neighbouring notes of a scale.
It is the smallest normal movement within that scale.
No scale note is skipped between the two pitches.
MC-0019.falseFeature: The melody skips one or more neighbouring scale notes.

trueFeatures: The steady beat is grouped into bars of two, three or four main beats.
The first beat of each bar is usually the strongest.
Common examples include 2/4, 3/4, 4/4, 6/8, 9/8 and 12/8.
MC-0020.falseFeature: It is a short decoration played around the end of a phrase on drum kit.

trueFeatures: A note or beat receives extra emphasis.
The sound may be made stronger or louder than those around it.
A > sign above or below a note is common notation for it.
MC-0021.falseFeature: A dot beneath a curved arch tells the performer to hold a note longer.

trueFeatures: It is a slow tempo marking.
The music should move at a calm, unhurried speed.
It is often associated with about 66-76 beats per minute.
MC-0022.falseFeature: It is a fast, lively marking often linked with 120-168 beats per minute.

trueFeatures: It is a fast and lively tempo marking.
The music should move with energy and forward motion.
It is often associated with about 120-168 beats per minute.
MC-0023.falseFeature: It is a slow marking linked with a calm, unhurried speed.

trueFeatures: It is the regular underlying pulse of the music.
A listener might tap or clap along with it.
Tempo tells us how quickly it moves.
MC-0024.falseFeature: It is a short rhythmic decoration played on a drum kit near a section change.

trueFeatures: It is a short rhythmic decoration played on a drum kit.
It often appears near the end of a phrase or section.
It may signal a change or add excitement.
MC-0025.falseFeature: It is the regular underlying pulse that a listener can tap along with.

trueFeatures: The tempo increases.
The beat moves more quickly than before.
The change may happen suddenly or gradually.
MC-0026.falseFeature: The beat moves less quickly than before.

trueFeatures: It has a strong, regular walking pulse.
It is usually organised in two or four beats per bar.
Clear accents and percussion often support coordinated movement.
MC-0027.falseFeature: It is a simple-triple dance with a ONE-two-three pattern.

trueFeatures: Notes or accents fall between or on weaker parts of the main beats.
It can create lift, surprise or syncopated energy.
In 4/4 it may emphasise beats 2 and 4 instead of beats 1 and 3.
MC-0028.falseFeature: Notes and accents line up directly with the main pulse.

trueFeatures: Notes or accents line up directly with the main pulse.
It reinforces the metre and often feels rhythmically stable.
In 4/4 it may strengthen beats 1 and 3.
MC-0029.falseFeature: Notes or accents fall between the main beats or on their weaker parts.

trueFeatures: A note or rest is held longer than its written value.
The performer or conductor decides the extra length.
Its symbol is a dot beneath a curved arch.
MC-0030.falseFeature: A > sign tells the performer to give a note extra emphasis.

trueFeatures: It is a lively Scottish dance in simple time.
It usually has two or four beats per bar and a quick, flowing pulse.
Fiddle, accordion or a Scottish dance band often performs it.
MC-0031.falseFeature: It is a triple-time dance with a strong ONE-two-three pattern.

trueFeatures: The tempo decreases.
The beat moves less quickly than before.
The change may happen suddenly or gradually.
MC-0032.falseFeature: The beat moves more quickly than before.

trueFeatures: It is a dance in simple triple time.
There are three beats in each bar.
The first beat is strongest, creating a ONE-two-three pattern.
MC-0033.falseFeature: It has a strong walking pulse with two or four beats per bar.

trueFeatures: A main voice or instrument receives musical support.
Other voices or instruments provide the support.
The supporting part may add harmony, rhythm or texture.
MC-0034.falseFeature: The performer has no supporting instruments or harmony parts.

trueFeatures: Two or more different notes sound at the same time.
Chords can create it.
Independent musical parts can combine to create it.
MC-0035.falseFeature: Every performer plays the same melody at the same pitch and time.

trueFeatures: The two notes have the same letter name.
One note is the next higher or lower version of the other.
The notes sound closely related at different pitch levels.
MC-0036.falseFeature: A three-note chord is called a triad.

trueFeatures: A short musical pattern or phrase repeats many times.
The repeated idea may contain rhythm, pitches or both.
A memorable repeated instrumental phrase is often called a riff.
MC-0037.falseFeature: A phrase repeats immediately at a different pitch level.

trueFeatures: All parts perform the same melody.
The parts begin that melody at different times.
The melody overlaps as each part continues and starts again.
MC-0038.falseFeature: All parts perform the same melody at the same pitch and at the same time.

trueFeatures: One voice or instrument takes the most prominent part.
It may be performed completely alone.
It may have accompaniment while remaining the featured line.
MC-0039.falseFeature: A whole organised group of singers performs together.

trueFeatures: A voice or instrument performs without supporting instruments.
No supporting harmony part is present.
For voices, the more advanced term is a cappella.
MC-0040.falseFeature: Other voices or instruments provide harmony, rhythm or texture underneath.

trueFeatures: Two or more performers play or sing the same melody.
They use the same pitch at the same time.
The result is one melodic line rather than harmony.
MC-0041.falseFeature: Parts begin the same melody at different times so that it overlaps.

trueFeatures: Opening and closing bellows push air through free reeds.
The right hand often plays keys or buttons.
The left hand often operates bass and chord buttons.
MC-0042.falseFeature: Felt-covered hammers strike strings when its keyboard keys are pressed.

trueFeatures: It usually has six strings.
Its hollow body amplifies vibrating strings without electricity.
It can be plucked or strummed with fingers or a plectrum.
MC-0043.falseFeature: It has a solid or semi-hollow body and needs an amplifier.

trueFeatures: An air-filled bag supplies the sound.
A chanter plays the melody while drone pipes sustain notes.
The chanter uses a double reed.
MC-0044.falseFeature: Its tuned metal playing areas are struck to produce melody, harmony and bass parts.

trueFeatures: The performer directs air into or across an instrument.
Vibrating lips can begin the sound on brass instruments.
An air edge or reed can begin the sound on woodwind instruments.
MC-0045.falseFeature: A hand, stick, mallet or beater hits the instrument to make it sound.

trueFeatures: A bow is drawn across an instrument's strings.
Friction between bow hair and string creates a sustained tone.
The Italian instruction arco tells a string player to use it.
MC-0046.falseFeature: A finger or plectrum pulls and releases the string.

trueFeatures: Buzzing lips make the sound through a cup-shaped or funnel-shaped mouthpiece.
Trumpet, French horn, trombone and tuba are members.
Lip tension, valves or a slide help to change pitch.
MC-0047.falseFeature: Sound begins by blowing across an opening or through a single or double reed.

trueFeatures: It is an organised group of singers.
The singers may perform in unison or in several voice parts.
It may sing with or without instrumental accompaniment.
MC-0048.falseFeature: Its members include trumpet, French horn, trombone and tuba.

trueFeatures: One performer plays a collection of drums and cymbals.
Sticks, brushes and foot pedals are used.
Bass drum, snare drum, tom-toms and hi-hat are common parts.
MC-0049.falseFeature: A keyboard makes felt-covered hammers strike tuned strings.

trueFeatures: Pickups turn string vibrations into an electrical signal.
It usually needs an amplifier.
Effects such as distortion may change its sound.
MC-0050.falseFeature: It is designed to make its full sound without pickups or electrical amplification.

trueFeatures: It is another name for a violin used in traditional or folk music.
It is especially associated with Scottish music.
It may be bowed or plucked.
MC-0051.falseFeature: It has an air-filled bag, a melody chanter and continuous drone pipes.

trueFeatures: It is a small ensemble performing traditional or folk music.
Acoustic instruments and voices are often used.
Fiddle, whistle, guitar, accordion or bagpipes may be included.
MC-0052.falseFeature: It is divided into strings, woodwind, brass and percussion sections.

trueFeatures: Notes are played or sung smoothly.
Each note connects to the next.
There is little or no audible gap between notes.
MC-0053.falseFeature: Notes are short and detached with clear gaps between them.

trueFeatures: It is a large instrumental ensemble.
Its four main families are strings, woodwind, brass and percussion.
Its balance and number of instruments can change with the music.
MC-0054.falseFeature: It is an organised group of singers performing together.

trueFeatures: Its keyboard can produce long sustained tones.
Pipe versions are strongly associated with churches.
Large examples may have several manuals and a pedalboard.
MC-0055.falseFeature: Felt-covered hammers strike strings whenever its keys are pressed.

trueFeatures: Instruments are sounded mainly by striking, shaking or scraping.
Some members produce definite, identifiable pitches.
Other members produce sounds with no fixed pitch.
MC-0056.falseFeature: Members make sound by buzzing the lips into a mouthpiece.

trueFeatures: Pressing a key makes a felt-covered hammer strike a string.
It can play melody and harmony together.
Changes in touch create a wide dynamic range.
MC-0057.falseFeature: Opening and closing bellows push air through free reeds.

trueFeatures: A string is pulled and released.
A finger, fingernail or plectrum may be used.
It creates a clear attack on guitar, harp or bowed strings.
MC-0058.falseFeature: Bow hair rubs against a string to make a sustained tone.

trueFeatures: It plays Scottish dance music for social dancing.
Accordion and fiddle are common members.
Piano, bass and drum kit may also be included.
MC-0059.falseFeature: It uses tuned steel pans to provide melody, harmony and bass parts.

trueFeatures: Notes are performed short and detached.
A clear gap separates one note from the next.
A dot above or below the note is common notation for it.
MC-0060.falseFeature: Notes connect smoothly with little or no audible gap.

trueFeatures: It is an ensemble of tuned steel pans.
It is associated with Trinidad and Tobago and the Caribbean.
Different pan sizes provide melody, harmony and bass parts.
MC-0061.falseFeature: Its players use accordions and fiddles for Scottish social dancing.

trueFeatures: A hand, stick, mallet or beater hits the instrument.
The impact begins the sound.
It is the main playing action for many percussion instruments.
MC-0062.falseFeature: Air is directed into or across an instrument to begin its sound.

trueFeatures: Violin, viola, cello, double bass and harp are members.
Vibrating strings produce the sound.
Bowing and plucking are common playing methods.
MC-0063.falseFeature: Sound begins by blowing across an opening or through a reed.

trueFeatures: A finger or plectrum sweeps across several strings quickly.
It usually sounds a chord on guitar or a similar instrument.
Several strings sound in one sweeping action.
MC-0064.falseFeature: One string is pulled and released to make a single clear attack.

trueFeatures: Its instruments produce definite, identifiable pitches.
They can play individual notes, scales or melodies.
Timpani, glockenspiel and xylophone are examples.
MC-0065.falseFeature: Its sounds have no fixed musical pitch and are heard mainly as rhythm.

trueFeatures: Its instruments have no fixed musical pitch.
Their sounds are heard mainly as rhythm and tone colour.
Snare drum, cymbals, triangle and tambourine are examples.
MC-0066.falseFeature: Its instruments can play named notes, scales and melodies.

trueFeatures: It is the human sound source for speaking and singing.
Vibrating vocal folds create its pitch.
The mouth and body resonances shape its sound.
MC-0067.falseFeature: Vibrating strings are amplified naturally by a hollow wooden body.

trueFeatures: Sound begins by blowing across an opening or through a reed.
Flute, oboe, clarinet, bassoon and saxophone are members.
Modern members may be made from wood or metal.
MC-0068.falseFeature: Buzzing lips make the sound through a metal mouthpiece.

trueFeatures: Layered percussion and repeated rhythmic patterns are common course features.
Call and response, singing and clapping may be heard.
Interlocking or overlapping rhythms and improvisation may be used.
MC-0069.falseFeature: The story is mainly sung on stage with costumes, scenery and an orchestra.

trueFeatures: Basso continuo and contrapuntal textures are common.
Ornaments, sequences and terraced dynamics are often used.
Harpsichord and organ are strongly associated with the style.
MC-0070.falseFeature: Rubato, chromatic harmony and a greatly expanded orchestra are common features.

trueFeatures: A solo instrument is contrasted with an orchestra.
The soloist has a prominent role.
It is usually divided into several movements.
MC-0071.falseFeature: A staged story is mainly sung with acting, costumes and scenery.

trueFeatures: It is a Scottish Gaelic vocal style also called puirt-a-beul.
Rhythmic Gaelic or nonsense syllables are sung.
It is usually unaccompanied and was used for dancing.
MC-0072.falseFeature: A big band performs riffs, swung rhythms and sections of brass and saxophones.

trueFeatures: It is a staged drama in which the story is mainly sung.
Soloists, chorus and an orchestra are used.
Acting, costumes, scenery and theatrical movement are included.
MC-0073.falseFeature: A jazz singer improvises with nonsense syllables instead of normal lyrics.

trueFeatures: A syncopated melody is often played on piano.
The accompaniment keeps a steady bass-and-chord pattern.
The left hand often alternates bass notes and chords.
MC-0074.falseFeature: It is a Jamaican popular style with a relaxed groove and off-beat guitar chords.

trueFeatures: Rhyming or rhythmically organised words are spoken.
The words are delivered in time to a beat.
Repeated grooves, samples and backing tracks may be used.
MC-0075.falseFeature: The full dramatic story is mainly sung on stage with an orchestra.

trueFeatures: It has a relaxed, steady groove and a prominent low bass line.
Short staccato guitar or keyboard chords fall on the off-beats.
Beats 2 and 4 are often stressed.
MC-0076.falseFeature: A piano plays a syncopated melody over alternating bass notes and chords.

trueFeatures: Expressive melodies, rubato and chromatic harmony are common.
A wide dynamic range and programmatic ideas may be used.
The orchestra is larger and includes a wider range of instruments.
MC-0077.falseFeature: Basso continuo, terraced dynamics and harpsichord are central features.

trueFeatures: It is a Scottish narrative song sung in Scots.
It tells a story and is often strophic.
It may be sung solo with simple accompaniment or unaccompanied.
MC-0078.falseFeature: It is a staged drama with acting, costumes, scenery and an orchestra.

trueFeatures: A big band often performs the style.
It uses a swung rhythmic feel, dance pulse, riffs and call and response.
Trumpet, trombone and saxophone sections play with a rhythm section.
MC-0079.falseFeature: It is an unaccompanied Gaelic vocal style using rhythmic nonsense syllables.

trueFeatures: The notes of a chord sound one after another.
The notes may rise, fall or follow another pattern.
An arpeggio is an ordered, flowing type of broken chord.
MC-0080.falseFeature: It is a sequence built from chords I, IV and V in a major key.

trueFeatures: The music moves from one key or tonal centre to another.
The scale relationships change.
The chord relationships change with the new key.
MC-0081.falseFeature: The music returns to the earlier tempo after a temporary speed change.

trueFeatures: It uses the tonic, subdominant and dominant chords of a major key.
The chords are built on scale degrees 1, 4 and 5.
In C major the chords are C major, F major and G major.
MC-0082.falseFeature: It is a five-note scale using scale degrees 1, 2, 3, 5 and 6.

trueFeatures: One note, or sometimes a fifth, sounds beneath changing music.
The supporting pitch may be sustained or repeatedly sounded.
Bagpipe drone pipes provide a familiar example.
MC-0083.falseFeature: The bass pitch changes repeatedly between a note and an off-beat chord.

trueFeatures: It is based on a major scale and major tonic chord.
It often sounds bright or stable, but it is not always happy.
Its scale pattern is tone-tone-semitone-tone-tone-tone-semitone.
MC-0084.falseFeature: Its natural, harmonic and melodic forms alter the sixth and seventh scale degrees.

trueFeatures: It is based on a minor scale and minor tonic chord.
It can sound darker or more tense than major, but is not always sad.
Natural, harmonic and melodic forms treat the sixth and seventh differently.
The notes have the same letter name.
They are eight scale degrees apart.
The upper note vibrates at twice the lower note's frequency.
MC-0086.falseFeature: It is a scale containing five different notes within the octave.

trueFeatures: It is a decorative note or group of notes added to a melody.
It adds expression or detail without changing the tune's basic outline.
Grace notes, trills, mordents and turns are examples.
MC-0087.falseFeature: It is a complete set of notes arranged in stepwise pitch order.

trueFeatures: A note is sustained or repeated in the bass.
The harmony changes above the bass note.
The note may fit some chords and clash with others.
MC-0088.falseFeature: Chord notes sound one after another as a rising or falling figure.

trueFeatures: It contains five different notes within the octave.
It is common in Scottish, Celtic, folk and world music.
A common major form uses scale degrees 1, 2, 3, 5 and 6.
MC-0089.falseFeature: Its notes have the same letter name and are eight scale degrees apart.

trueFeatures: It is a set of notes arranged in pitch order.
Its notes move stepwise upwards or downwards.
It provides pitch material for melodies and chords.
MC-0090.falseFeature: It is a decorative note or flourish added to an existing melody.

trueFeatures: It is improvised jazz singing.
Nonsense syllables and vocal sounds replace normal lyrics.
The singer invents ideas and may imitate instruments.
MC-0091.falseFeature: Rhyming words are spoken rhythmically in time with a beat.

trueFeatures: Music is organised around a central key or tonic.
At this level it mainly contrasts major and minor.
Scale notes and chords create a pull between dominant and tonic.
MC-0092.falseFeature: A bass note alternates with an off-beat chord in a repeated accompaniment.

trueFeatures: It is a repeated accompaniment pattern.
A bass note on the beat often alternates with a chord off the beat.
It is common in ragtime, popular and Scottish dance music.
MC-0093.falseFeature: One bass note stays or repeats while several different harmonies pass above it.

trueFeatures: It tells performers to return to the earlier tempo.
It follows a temporary speed change.
It restores the previous or main pace of the music.
MC-0094.falseFeature: It tells performers to make the tempo gradually faster.

trueFeatures: The tempo becomes gradually faster.
The beat speeds up over a passage.
It is a gradual change rather than an immediate jump.
MC-0095.falseFeature: It tells performers to return to the previous tempo after a change.

trueFeatures: One or more notes come before the first complete bar.
The notes lead into the first strong beat of a phrase.
It is also called an upbeat or pickup.
MC-0096.falseFeature: A note stays or repeats in the bass while the harmony changes above it.

trueFeatures: It means a moderate walking pace.
It is calmer than allegro and faster than adagio.
It is often associated with about 76-108 beats per minute.
MC-0097.falseFeature: It tells performers to make the tempo gradually faster.

trueFeatures: Each main beat divides into three equal parts.
6/8, 9/8 and 12/8 have two, three and four main beats.
Each main beat is often shown by a dotted crotchet.
MC-0098.falseFeature: Each main beat divides naturally into two equal parts.

trueFeatures: Dotted notes create unequal long-short or short-long patterns.
A dot lengthens a note by half of its original value.
A dotted quaver followed by a semiquaver is a common example.
MC-0099.falseFeature: It is a three-part musical form with sections in the order A-B-A.

trueFeatures: It is a lively Scottish or Irish dance.
It is often in 6/8 with two main beats per bar.
Each dotted-crotchet beat divides into three quavers.
MC-0100.falseFeature: It is a moderate Scottish dance in simple quadruple time with Scotch snaps.

trueFeatures: The tempo becomes gradually slower.
The slowing usually happens over several beats or bars.
It is often shortened to rall. in notation.
MC-0101.falseFeature: The tempo becomes gradually faster over a passage.

trueFeatures: A short accented note is followed by a longer note.
It is often written as a semiquaver followed by a dotted quaver.
It is strongly associated with the Scottish strathspey.
MC-0102.falseFeature: A dotted quaver is followed by a shorter semiquaver.

trueFeatures: Each main beat divides naturally into two equal parts.
It may have two, three or four crotchet beats in a bar.
2/4, 3/4 and 4/4 are common examples.
MC-0103.falseFeature: Each main beat divides naturally into three equal parts.

trueFeatures: It is a Scottish dance in simple quadruple time.
It is usually played at a moderate tempo.
Dotted rhythms and the Scotch snap are important features.
MC-0104.falseFeature: It is a lively dance in compound 6/8 with two dotted-crotchet beats.

trueFeatures: Emphasis falls on weak beats or off-beats.
A note may be held across a strong beat.
The expected accent pattern is displaced, adding drive or surprise.
MC-0105.falseFeature: The tempo gradually slows over several beats or bars.

trueFeatures: It is a prominent solo passage in a concerto or similar work.
It displays the performer's expressive and technical skill.
It often appears near a movement's end and may be unaccompanied.
MC-0106.falseFeature: It is a contrasting popular-song section traditionally about eight bars long.

trueFeatures: One part begins a melody and another enters later with the same melody.
The parts continue together so the melody overlaps.
Following parts reproduce the melody exactly.
MC-0107.falseFeature: The copied musical idea may be altered and does not need to continue throughout.

trueFeatures: A musical idea is copied shortly afterwards by another part.
The copy may be exact or altered.
The copying does not need to continue for the whole piece.
MC-0108.falseFeature: Every following part must reproduce the complete melody exactly as it overlaps.

trueFeatures: It is a contrasting section in a popular song.
It often follows repeated verses or choruses.
Its name is used even when it is not exactly eight bars long.
MC-0109.falseFeature: It is a solo concerto passage designed to display a performer's skill.

trueFeatures: It has three sections in the order A-B-A.
The middle B section contrasts with the opening.
The final A returns and may be exact or slightly changed.
MC-0110.falseFeature: Verses change their words while a chorus returns with the same words and music.

trueFeatures: A main melody or idea is stated first.
The idea returns several times in changed forms.
Rhythm, harmony, texture, tempo or instruments may change.
MC-0111.falseFeature: The structure is always an opening A, a contrasting B and an unchanged final A.

trueFeatures: Verses repeat the same music with changing words.
A contrasting chorus returns with the same or similar words and music.
The chorus usually contains the main hook.
MC-0112.falseFeature: The complete form is always an opening A, contrasting B and returning A section.

trueFeatures: It is the lowest standard female voice or choral part.
It lies below soprano.
It forms the A in SATB.
MC-0113.falseFeature: It is the highest standard female voice and carries the top SATB line.

trueFeatures: One or more singers support the lead vocalist.
They may add harmony or repeated responses.
They may sing sustained notes in the background.
MC-0114.falseFeature: The singers form a Scottish Gaelic style that is usually unaccompanied.

trueFeatures: It is the lowest standard adult male voice.
It provides the lowest vocal line in SATB writing.
Its line often supports the harmony.
MC-0115.falseFeature: It is a high adult male voice that forms the T in SATB.

trueFeatures: It is a large, low-pitched untuned drum.
It is struck with a beater and provides weight or strong accents.
In a drum kit it is operated by a foot pedal.
MC-0116.falseFeature: Metal wires beneath its lower head create a crisp rattling sound.

trueFeatures: It is a low-pitched electric string instrument.
It usually has four strings and is played by plucking or with a plectrum.
It supplies bass lines and usually needs amplification.
MC-0117.falseFeature: A telescoping slide changes pitch instead of valves.

trueFeatures: It contains brass instruments and percussion.
It usually has no woodwind or orchestral strings.
Cornets, tenor horns, euphoniums, trombones and tubas are common.
MC-0118.falseFeature: Flutes, clarinets and saxophones form an important part of the ensemble.

trueFeatures: It is a low orchestral string instrument.
It is larger than a viola but smaller than a double bass.
It rests on an endpin between the knees and is usually bowed.
MC-0119.falseFeature: It is held under the chin and is the highest standard orchestral string instrument.

trueFeatures: It is a single-reed woodwind instrument.
It has a wide range and a smooth, flexible tone.
It appears in orchestras, wind bands and jazz ensembles.
MC-0120.falseFeature: It is sounded by blowing across an embouchure hole without a reed.

trueFeatures: They are round metal untuned percussion instruments.
They may be struck together or hit with sticks or mallets.
They can create crashes, accents, rolls and shimmering sounds.
MC-0121.falseFeature: Metal wires beneath a drum head produce a crisp rattling sound.

trueFeatures: It electronically alters and clips an amplified signal.
It creates a rough, fuzzy or aggressive tone.
It is especially linked with electric guitar in rock music.
MC-0122.falseFeature: A mute changes an instrument's tone, often making it softer or more distant.

trueFeatures: It is the largest orchestral string instrument.
It is the lowest-pitched member of the orchestral string family.
It may be played with a bow or by plucking.
MC-0123.falseFeature: It is the smallest and highest-pitched standard orchestral string instrument.

trueFeatures: It is a high woodwind instrument that is usually made of metal.
The player blows across an embouchure hole.
It makes sound without using a reed.
MC-0124.falseFeature: A single reed attached to a mouthpiece begins the sound.

trueFeatures: It is a tuned percussion instrument with metal bars.
Its bars are arranged like a keyboard and struck with hard beaters.
It has a bright, high and bell-like sound.
MC-0125.falseFeature: Its wooden bars create a dry, sharply defined tone.

trueFeatures: It is an untuned scraper with a ridged surface.
It is traditionally made from a hollow gourd or wood.
A stick is drawn over the ridges to make a rasping sound.
MC-0126.falseFeature: Pairs of metal jingles sound when a frame drum is shaken or tapped.

trueFeatures: It is a large plucked string instrument with strings of different lengths.
A full-size orchestral example usually has pedals that alter its strings.
It can play chords, arpeggios and glissandi.
MC-0127.falseFeature: Its strings are plucked mechanically whenever keyboard keys are pressed.

trueFeatures: Pressing its keys makes a mechanism pluck strings.
It has a bright, crisp tone and is strongly linked with Baroque music.
It offers much less dynamic control than a piano.
MC-0128.falseFeature: Its strings are plucked directly by a player's fingers and its pedals alter pitch.

trueFeatures: A device changes an instrument's normal tone.
The sound may become softer, thinner, more nasal or more distant.
The exact effect depends on the instrument and the device used.
MC-0129.falseFeature: An electronic signal is deliberately clipped to make a rough, fuzzy tone.

trueFeatures: It consists of several tubes of different lengths joined together.
Each tube produces a different pitch.
The player blows across the open tops of the tubes.
MC-0130.falseFeature: A whistle-like mouthpiece directs air into a single tube with finger holes.

trueFeatures: It is an end-blown woodwind instrument with finger holes.
It uses a whistle-like mouthpiece.
Descant, treble, tenor and bass are its main sizes.
MC-0131.falseFeature: The player blows across the open tops of several joined tubes of different lengths.

trueFeatures: It is a single-reed woodwind instrument usually made of brass.
It has a powerful, flexible tone.
It is prominent in jazz, wind bands and some popular music.
MC-0132.falseFeature: It is sounded by blowing across an embouchure hole and uses no reed.

trueFeatures: It is an untuned drum with metal wires beneath its lower head.
The wires produce a crisp rattling sound.
It is played with sticks or brushes in orchestras, bands and drum kits.
MC-0133.falseFeature: It is a large low drum whose drum-kit version is played with a foot pedal.

trueFeatures: It is the highest standard female voice or choral part.
It usually carries the top line in choral writing.
It forms the S in SATB.
MC-0134.falseFeature: It is the lowest standard female voice and lies below the soprano.

trueFeatures: It is a frame drum with pairs of metal jingles.
It may be shaken, tapped or struck by hand.
It is usually classed as untuned percussion.
MC-0135.falseFeature: A stick scrapes a ridged gourd or wooden surface to make a rasping sound.

trueFeatures: It is a high adult male voice or choral part.
It lies above baritone and bass.
It forms the T in SATB.
MC-0136.falseFeature: It is the lowest adult male voice and supplies the bottom SATB line.

trueFeatures: They are large tuned kettle drums with bowl-shaped shells.
A pedal can adjust each drum's pitch.
They can reinforce harmony as well as rhythm.
MC-0137.falseFeature: Metal wires stretched beneath the lower drum head make a rattling sound.

trueFeatures: It is an open triangular shape made from a bent metal bar.
It hangs freely and is struck with a metal beater.
It produces a bright, ringing untuned sound.
MC-0138.falseFeature: Metal bars arranged like a keyboard play definite, high pitches.

trueFeatures: It is a low-to-middle brass instrument.
A telescoping slide usually changes its pitch.
It has a powerful tone and can make smooth glissandi.
MC-0139.falseFeature: It is the highest common orchestral brass instrument and usually has three valves.

trueFeatures: It is the highest common orchestral brass instrument.
It uses a cup-shaped mouthpiece and usually has three valves.
It has a bright, penetrating tone.
MC-0140.falseFeature: A telescoping slide usually changes its pitch and allows smooth glissandi.

trueFeatures: It is the smallest standard orchestral string instrument.
It is the highest-pitched standard member of its family.
It is held under the chin and usually played with a bow.
MC-0141.falseFeature: It is a low string instrument supported by an endpin and held between the knees.

trueFeatures: It contains woodwind, brass and percussion instruments.
It usually has no orchestral string section.
Flute, clarinet, saxophone, trumpet and trombone may be included.
MC-0142.falseFeature: It contains brass and percussion only, with no woodwind instruments.

trueFeatures: It is a tuned percussion instrument with wooden bars.
Its bars are arranged like a keyboard and struck with beaters.
It has a dry, bright and sharply defined tone.
MC-0143.falseFeature: Its metal bars create a very high, bell-like sound.
